title: AI data centres a threat to grid: AGL
full_text:
AGL Energy has warned a surge in electricity demand from data centres could outstrip official forecasts, intensifying pressure on an already finely balanced power grid and sharpening the need for new investment in firming capacity.
Speaking at the Macquarie Australia Conference on Tuesday, chief executive Damien Nicks said a wave of AI-led data centre developments – concentrated in NSW, Victoria and the ACT – was emerging as a “material” driver of long-term electricity demand, alongside electrification and electric vehicles.
The scale of projects in the pipeline, he said, had the potential to exceed expectations set out by the Australian Energy Market Operator.
The comments underscore a growing tension at the heart of Australia’s energy transition: while renewable generation continues to expand, a rapid build-out of energy-intensive digital infrastructure is set to place fresh strain on a system already operating with limited margin for error.
AGL estimates operational data centres currently account for about 5 terawatt hours of electricity demand in the National Electricity Market – roughly equivalent to the annual power consumption of about 900,000 homes – but this could rise sharply as projects under construction come online and a deeper development pipeline is realised. If all proposed projects proceed, demand linked to data centres could reach as much as 34 terawatt hours – roughly equivalent to the annual electricity consumption of about 6 million homes and exceeding central forecasts and what would materially reshape the demand outlook.
While Mr Nicks acknowledged not all projects would ultimately proceed, he said the direction of travel was clear.
“The outlook and opportunity is significant,” he told investors, pointing to strong and durable demand tailwinds driven by digitisation and the rapid uptake of artificial-intelligence technologies.
Labor is keen to foster data centres and their economic benefits, but with the energy grid struggling to transition as quickly as Anthony Albanese has pledged, developers are coming under mounting pressure to secure their own zero-emissions power.
In practice, that has translated into an expectation that hyperscale operators will underwrite new generation – typically through long-term renewable power purchase agreements, co-located solar and battery projects, or dedicated firming arrangements – rather than relying solely on existing grid capacity.
Industry executives say the shift reflects the reality of a system with limited headroom, where adding large, continuous demand without new supply risks amplifying volatility and reliability concerns. While there is no formal requirement to build their own infrastructure, developers are increasingly being compelled to fund it indirectly.
The data centre build-out is also beginning to reshape gas markets, with suppliers reporting a surge in demand for firming capacity as developers look to secure reliable power.
Beach Energy chief executive Brett Woods said proponents were increasingly being forced to underwrite new supply, warning large loads could not simply draw on existing capacity.
“You can’t be a net taker if you’re going to build a data centre … you’ve got to have additional power supply,” Mr Woods said, adding that gas remained the only scalable firming solution currently available.
“We’re seeing a lot of inbound demand from data centres … looking for firm power,” he said, though he cautioned the scale of projects under discussion remained uncertain. The prospect of a step-change in electricity consumption comes as the grid grapples with shifting demand patterns and rising peak loads, particularly during winter when solar output is lower.
Mr Nicks said the market remained “finely balanced”, with reliability increasingly dependent on the alignment of variable renewable generation, thermal plant availability and interconnector flows.
AGL on Wednesday also narrowed its earnings guidance for the 2026 financial year, citing strong operational performance and improved plant availability.
The company now expects underlying net profit of between $610m and $680m, up from a previous range of $580m-$680m. But it said conditions could soften into 2027, with lower wholesale electricity prices and broader economic uncertainty expected to weigh on earnings.

summary: AGL Energy has raised concerns that the increasing electricity demand from AI-driven data centres may exceed official forecasts, putting additional strain on Australia's power grid. The company estimates that operational data centres currently consume about 5 terawatt hours of electricity, a figure that could rise significantly with new projects. This situation highlights the need for new investments in energy generation to support the growing demand from digital infrastructure.

heading: Data Science and AI
key_points: AGL warns that data centres could drive electricity demand beyond current forecasts.
Operational data centres currently consume about 5 terawatt hours of electricity, potentially rising to 34 terawatt hours.
Developers are increasingly expected to secure their own renewable energy sources to support new data centre projects.
relevance: This is relevant to actuaries and financial services professionals as it underscores the financial implications of energy demand trends and the need for investment in infrastructure.
